     and should not be removed. -->

# Reflection — TickerLens

> **Note:** This feedback is AI-generated and experimental. It's meant to surface useful observations, not to grade you. Take what resonates, ignore what doesn't. Your own sense of what you learned matters more than anything here.

## Scope & Idea Clarity

**What went well:** `scope.md` is sharp and opinionated. The user is specifically named ("individual investors like Kamen — people who invest based on intuition and common sense in companies they believe in"), the inspiration section does comparative analysis with real products (Incite AI, VectorVest, WarrenAI) naming what's wrong with each and how TickerLens differs, and the cuts are real — "no portfolio tracking," "no chat interface" (explicitly walking back earlier brainstorming), "no charts." Cuts that contradict an earlier impulse are the most valuable kind; they prove a real decision was made rather than scope-by-omission.

**What to try next time:** The "who it's for" is honest but narrow — it's you. That's a fine hackathon move, but scope docs get stronger when the user definition survives a "would someone unlike me also want this?" pressure test. One sentence about the adjacent user would widen the aperture without diluting focus.

## Requirements Thinking

**What went well:** PRD acceptance criteria are concretely testable — not "smooth UX" but "transitions between states are smooth fades/animations, never jarring cuts" and "input field retains the entered text so the user can edit rather than retype." The Open Questions section is mature: "Source link presentation format — inline citations or reference list?" marked as "Can wait until build" defers the decision to where real signal (actual Opus output) will arrive. That's experienced PRD writing.

**What to try next time:** The "implicit connections" criterion is load-bearing for the whole product but not testable as written: "Arguments surface implicit connections rather than just restating headlines." Opus delivered on it (the AAPL Sell case's dual-squeeze thesis is real), but the criterion has no failure mode. A sharper version: "At least one of the three arguments must surface a non-obvious cross-domain connection (e.g., regulation → revenue stream) that isn't present in any single source headline." Testable by reading the output.

## Technical Decisions

**What went well:** The spec traces cleanly to requirements. "No hard cuts" in the PRD → SSE pacing wrapper + mid-stream header emit. "Implicit connections" → `web_search` attached to the news agent, not synthesis. "Three distinct perspectives that disagree" → single Opus call with forced `emit_briefing` tool rather than three parallel calls. Every major architecture decision has a readable line back to the PRD. The checklist sequencing (backend-first inside-out, stubs before real LLMs, SSE pacing tested before LLM latency enters the picture) also shows thinking about *risk*, not just dependency order — the risky learning surfaces got stress-tested first.

**What to try next time:** The spec's 30s news-agent timeout turned out to be wrong under real conditions (web_search needs 60-90s cold). Normal drift — specs can't predict vendor latency without data — but worth flagging in the spec itself as "to be tuned during build" rather than committed to. A small convention: spec assumptions that depend on empirical data get a `TBT` (to be tuned) marker. Keeps the spec honest about what's decided vs. what's a placeholder.

## Plan vs. Reality

**What went well:** The build landed very close to the spec. Every PRD user story has a corresponding implementation. The three-perspectives hook works (the AAPL Sell case's implicit connection is genuinely non-obvious). The mid-stream header emit — the hardest piece, and nearly broke — got diagnosed and fixed during live verification, and the fix matches what the spec said all along (it was the initial implementation that was wrong, not the spec). Drift was minimal and every drift point was caught: the 30s → 90s timeout, scope-creep error-routing pulled back out of step 5, dividend heuristic bug caught on VUSA, HTML-encoded names on ETF tickers.

**What to try next time:** Step 10 ("polish pass") turned out to be smaller than the checklist implied because steps 8/9a/9b were already well-polished inline. Not a failure, but a signal that step 10 was under-specified relative to earlier steps. When a checklist item is mostly human tasks (recording, uploading, submitting), the entry should say so explicitly rather than implying code work remains.

## How You Worked

**What went well:** This was the strongest dimension. Actively shaped the build throughout rather than passively accepting outputs.

- **Step 5:** caught the mid-stream emit bug during live verification ("header and summary emitted almost at the same time") that the initial test didn't cover. Senior-engineer verification — reading the *quality* of the output, not just "did it run."
- **Step 6:** when the news agent hit the 30s timeout, asked for debug logging instead of bumping the number. "See what's actually happening" over "make the error go away" is a habit most people skip.
- **Step 7:** read the full AAPL briefing substantively (not just "three blobs came back") and implicitly flagged the moderate-everywhere calibration question by sharing raw output for critique.
- **Step 9a:** stress-tested with VUSA and surfaced two real bugs (dividend yield heuristic, HTML-encoded names) that AAPL would never have exposed.

Comprehension checks: answered correctly on first try at steps 5, 6, 7 with connective reasoning — not just naming the mechanism, but connecting it to *why* it matters. That's internalization, not memorization.

**What to try next time:** Genuinely hard to find a growth area here — your engagement was at the high end of what this process can absorb. One small thing: at step 7, you flagged the "all moderate" calibration observation implicitly by sharing the payload. Making that explicit ("I think Sell earned strong — should we tune the prompt?") would have either triggered a fix or a conscious decision to ship as-is. Flagging-but-deferring is valid; making the deferral explicit keeps it from becoming silent drift.

## Your Goals

Stated goal: *"expand knowledge in agent orchestration — move from building individual AI components to coordinating and orchestrating multi-agent systems."*

Where it came through:
- **LangGraph topology fluency** — working mental models for `add_conditional_edges` fan-out, `stream_mode=["updates","custom"]` for mid-node emits, and how `state.error` routes through the graph. Multi-agent coordination patterns you can re-use.
- **Evidence/reasoning split** — giving `web_search` to the news agent but *not* to synthesis (news gathers, Opus reasons) is the kind of separation-of-concerns move that distinguishes real multi-agent architecture from "one big agent with many tools." Comprehension check at step 6 showed full internalization.
- **Forced structured output** — using `tool_choice` to force `emit_briefing` / `emit_news`, plus Pydantic validation on the tool output, is a pattern you'll use in every serious agent app from now on.

Where to push next: your current graph is a DAG — fan-out, fan-in, done. The next frontier is cyclic graphs with reflection (a critic node that sends work back to synthesis if confidence is thin, or a validator that re-runs the news agent with a refined query). That's where "orchestration" starts to mean something beyond "dispatch." If you run a v2 of TickerLens or a new agent project, add one cycle and see how it changes your mental model.

## Reflection

**On meeting the goal:** Self-assessed as reached — with clarity on what's learned (graph wiring, streaming, structured output, separation of concerns) and clarity on what's next (advancing from here). Confidence in what's internalized plus recognition that there's more to learn is the useful place to land after one project. Overconfidence after one project is the failure mode; you're not in it.

**What surprised you most:** How quickly everything came together.

The reason is worth naming, because it's the thing that carries forward. The build felt fast because the planning made it fast — by the time you hit `/build`, every decision had already been made. No "wait, should the news agent also do synthesis?" mid-build. No "what should the error state look like?" while mid-SSE-wiring. The checklist gave each session one small, fully-specified target, and the docs resolved every ambiguity the agent would have otherwise asked you about.

The counterintuitive thing: the *total* time probably wasn't less than building without SDD would have taken. But the *felt* time was less, because the planning work doesn't feel like "real work" the way coding does, and the build phase had no friction. That's the compounding return on structured planning — slow upfront thinking trades for fast downstream execution, and execution is where frustration usually lives.

**On running the process again:** Honest answer — not enough data yet. Reflection needs reps. The things you'd change on run #2 are usually invisible until you hit the same decision point a second time and notice your instinct differs. Worth revisiting this after the next SDD project.
